title: 1. Navigation (pwd)
definition: In Linux, your current active directory location is referred to as the Working Directory.
explanation: The pwd (print working directory) command prints your absolute filesystem path from the root directory down to your current folder.
instruction: Run the pwd command in the terminal to inspect your current directory.

title: 2. Working Directories (ls)
definition: Linux filesystems are built of directory folders containing files and configurations.
explanation: The ls command lists directory contents. Adding the -a flag includes hidden files (files starting with a dot, like .bashrc) in the output.
instruction: List all contents inside your home directory including hidden configurations using ls -a.

title: 3. Files (touch)
definition: Creating files is a fundamental task in development and operations.
explanation: The touch command creates a new empty file at the path you specify, or updates the modified timestamp of an existing file.
instruction: Create an empty text file named note.txt using touch.

title: 4. Directories (mkdir)
definition: Folders or directories are used to structure, group, and isolate files logically.
explanation: The mkdir (make directory) command creates a brand new directory folder in your current path.
instruction: Create a directory named backup in your home path.

title: 5. Copy (cp)
definition: Copying duplicates files, keeping the original source file intact.
explanation: The cp command copies files from a source path to a destination path, letting you make backups or template copies.
instruction: Copy note.txt into the backup directory under the name note_copy.txt.
expected: Copy is generated inside the backup subfolder.
hint: Run exact command: cp note.txt backup/note_copy.txt

title: 6. Move (mv)
definition: Moving relocates a file to a new path, deleting it from its original location.
explanation: The mv command transfers files from one folder to another. The original source file ceases to exist in its old location.
instruction: Move the note_copy.txt file back to your home directory /home/student.
expected: note_copy.txt resides in /home/student, no longer in backup/.

title: 7. Rename (mv)
definition: Renaming updates the name of a file while keeping it in the same directory.
explanation: In Linux, renaming files is also done using the mv command by simply specifying a new filename as the destination.
instruction: Rename note_copy.txt in your home directory to log.txt.
hint: Run exact command: mv note_copy.txt log.txt

title: 8. Delete (rm)
definition: Removing files permanently frees up storage space on the disk.
explanation: The rm (remove) command deletes files instantly and permanently. Note that Linux has no Recycle Bin to restore files from!
instruction: Delete the original note.txt file.
expected: note.txt is removed from your filesystem.

title: 9. Viewing Files (cat)
definition: Viewing text file contents is essential to check logs and configurations.
explanation: The cat (concatenate) command reads contents of text files and prints them directly to the console stream.
instruction: Print the contents of log.txt using cat.
expected: Blank output or contents printed to stdout.

title: 10. Permissions (chmod)
definition: Linux filesystems secure files using read (r), write (w), and execute (x) user permission flags.
explanation: The chmod command updates file mode permissions. Octal permissions like '600' restrict file access to the owner only.
instruction: Restrict log.txt permissions to owner read/write only (600).

title: 11. Users (id)
definition: Linux accounts are identified by numerical IDs (UIDs) for security.
explanation: The id command details the active user account identifiers, primary group, and other joined security groups.
instruction: Inspect your active identity information using id.

title: 12. Groups (groups)
definition: Groups categorize users together to simplify filesystem security controls.
explanation: The groups command prints a list of all user group memberships for the current user session.
instruction: Check what groups you are associated with.

title: 13. Searching (grep)
definition: Searching allows locating lines matching specific text patterns within large files.
explanation: The grep command filters files for string matches. It prints only matching lines containing the search pattern.
instruction: Search for the keyword 'student' in log.txt using grep.
hint: Run exact command: grep student log.txt

title: 14. Pipes (|)
definition: Piping is the redirecting of output streams to form custom tools chains.
explanation: Pipes (|) send output of one command as the input to another command, enabling complex command chains.
instruction: Pipe directory files list to grep to locate log.txt.
hint: Run exact command: ls | grep log.txt

title: 15. Redirection (>)
definition: Redirection writes command results directly into file paths.
explanation: Redirection operators write stdout directly into files. '>' overwrites a file; '>>' appends text.
instruction: Write the text 'DevOps' directly into a new file named dynamic.txt.

title: 16. Environment Variables
definition: Environment variables configure system applications dynamically.
explanation: Environment variables store config states globally. Use 'export KEY=VALUE' to set variables.
instruction: Export an environment variable named REGISTRY with value 'local'.
expected: REGISTRY=local is visible in printenv outputs.

title: 17. Processes (ps)
definition: Processes represent running program instances on the machine.
explanation: The ps command lists active process instances running under the current terminal shell.
instruction: View running system processes using ps.
expected: List showing bash and ps processes.

title: 18. Networking (ip route)
definition: Networking configurations direct internet traffic packets to hosts.
explanation: DevOps requires networking tasks. The ip route command views active gateways routing tables.
instruction: Inspect routing configurations using ip route.

title: 19. Directory Deletions
definition: Deleting directories requires removing all children and files within them recursively.
explanation: To delete directories containing files, use rm with recursive (-r) and force (-f) flags.
instruction: Recursively delete the backup directory.

title: 20. Workspace Cleanup
definition: Cleaning temporary files is a standard step after finishing maintenance operations.
explanation: Finally, check your workspace directories status. Only log.txt and dynamic.txt should remain.
instruction: Execute ls -la to check final directories state.
expected: backup directory is gone, note.txt is gone.
